words.shortDefinition: Google's open-source high-performance JavaScript and WebAssembly engine.
words.longDefinition: Written in C++, V8 compiles JavaScript directly to native machine code before executing it, using JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation. It is used in Google Chrome and Node.js.
words.techContext: Understanding V8's optimization strategies (like hidden classes and inline caching) is key for writing highly performant JavaScript.
words.exampleSentence: Since Node.js is built on the V8 engine, it benefits from the same rapid JavaScript execution as the Chrome browser.
words.etymology: Named after the V8 internal combustion engine, implying high power and speed.

words.shortDefinition: The sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into pixels.
words.longDefinition: The process includes DOM construction, CSSOM construction, Render Tree creation, Layout, and Painting. Optimizing the CRP is essential for improving frontend performance and reducing Time to First Paint.
words.techContext: Developers optimize the CRP by minimizing render-blocking resources and prioritizing visible content.
words.exampleSentence: By inlining critical CSS, we reduced the Critical Rendering Path and improved our Lighthouse performance score.

codeExample.code:
<!-- Link tags with 'rel=stylesheet' block CRP -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">

<!-- 'async' or 'defer' prevents scripts from blocking CRP -->
<script async src="app.js"></script>

words.shortDefinition: The mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking I/O operations.
words.longDefinition: Despite being single-threaded, JS can handle async tasks by offloading them to the browser APIs. The Event Loop constantly monitors the Call Stack and the Callback Queue; if the stack is empty, it pushes the first task from the queue to the stack.
words.techContext: Essential for understanding how 'setTimeout' or 'Promises' actually execute and why they don't freeze the UI.
words.exampleSentence: The Event Loop ensures that even during a heavy network request, the browser remains responsive to user clicks.
words.etymology: Descriptive of a continuous loop checking for events to process.

words.shortDefinition: A script that runs in the background, independent of other scripts.
words.longDefinition: Web Workers allow you to run JavaScript in a background thread, separate from the main execution thread of a web application. This prevents heavy computations from blocking the UI thread (preventing 'jank').
words.techContext: Used for data processing, image manipulation, or complex calculations that would otherwise freeze the browser tab.
words.exampleSentence: I moved the CSV parsing logic into a Web Worker so the user can still interact with the dashboard while the data loads.

words.shortDefinition: The browser process of recalculating the positions and geometries of elements.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Layout. It happens whenever a change affects the layout of the page (e.g., changing width, height, or font size). Reflow is a computationally expensive operation because it often triggers reflows of parent and sibling elements.
words.techContext: Developers try to minimize reflows by batching DOM changes or using CSS properties that only trigger compositing (like transform).
words.exampleSentence: Resizing the browser window triggers a global reflow as every element's dimensions must be recomputed.

words.shortDefinition: The browser process of redrawing elements to the screen without changing their layout.
words.longDefinition: Happens when changes are made to an element's appearance that do not affect its layout (e.g., color, visibility, or background-image). While cheaper than reflow, frequent repaints can still impact performance and battery life.
words.techContext: Often follows a reflow, but can happen independently. Optimizing for 60fps requires minimizing both reflows and repaints.
words.exampleSentence: Changing the button's background color on hover triggers a repaint but not a reflow.

words.shortDefinition: A data structure that records where in the program we are currently executing.
words.longDefinition: JavaScript uses a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) stack to manage function execution. When a function is called, it's pushed onto the stack; when it returns, it's popped off. If the stack grows too large, a 'Stack Overflow' occurs.
words.techContext: The 'synchronous' part of JavaScript's execution model. It must be empty for the event loop to process callbacks.
words.exampleSentence: I looked at the call stack in the debugger to trace which function was responsible for the error.

words.shortDefinition: Just-In-Time compilation; converting code to machine instructions during execution.
words.longDefinition: A method of improving the performance of interpreted programs. The JIT compiler monitors code as it runs; frequently executed code ('hot' code) is compiled into highly optimized machine code to speed up future executions.
words.techContext: The reason modern JavaScript is significantly faster than old versions. Engines like V8 use multiple JIT tiers (e.g., Ignition and TurboFan).
words.exampleSentence: The JIT compiler optimized our loops so effectively that the JavaScript version ran nearly as fast as native C++.

words.shortDefinition: The object representation of an HTML document structure.
words.longDefinition: Document Object Model Tree. It is constructed by the browser as it parses HTML. Each tag becomes a node, and the hierarchy reflects the nested structure of the markup. It is the target for JavaScript DOM APIs.
words.techContext: The starting point of the rendering pipeline. Large DOM trees can slow down performance and increase memory usage.
words.exampleSentence: The browser builds the DOM tree incrementally as it receives chunks of HTML over the network.
words.etymology: Acronym for Document Object Model + Tree (data structure).

codeExample.code: Hello

words.shortDefinition: The object representation of the styles associated with the DOM.
words.longDefinition: CSS Object Model Tree. Constructed by the browser while parsing CSS. It maps styles to DOM nodes. CSSOM construction is render-blocking because the browser cannot render the page until it knows the styles for each element.
words.techContext: Used alongside the DOM tree to create the Render Tree. Understanding CSSOM is vital for CSS performance optimization.
words.exampleSentence: Complex CSS selectors can make the CSSOM tree construction slower, delaying the initial paint of the page.

words.shortDefinition: A combination of DOM and CSSOM that includes only visible elements.
words.longDefinition: The result of combining the DOM and CSSOM trees. It contains all visible DOM content and the computed styles for each node. Elements with 'display: none' are excluded from the Render Tree (but 'visibility: hidden' elements are included).
words.techContext: The blueprint used for the Layout phase. It represents exactly what will eventually be drawn on the screen.
words.exampleSentence: Since the modal is hidden with 'display: none', it isn't part of the Render Tree until the user clicks the open button.

words.shortDefinition: The process of combining different layers of a page to produce the final image.
words.longDefinition: The browser splits the page into layers (like stacking sheets of glass). After painting the layers, the compositor thread combines them. This is often done on the GPU for better performance.
words.techContext: Animations that only trigger compositing (like 'opacity' or 'transform') are 'GPU accelerated' and much smoother than those triggering layout or paint.
words.exampleSentence: By using 'will-change: transform', we forced the element onto its own compositor layer to ensure a buttery-smooth 60fps animation.

words.shortDefinition: The process of turning vector-based elements into pixels.
words.longDefinition: The browser takes the paint records (list of draw calls) and fills in the pixels. This happens after the Layout and Paint phases but before (or during) Compositing. Modern browsers use GPU rasterization for speed.
words.techContext: Large images or complex SVG filters can make rasterization a bottleneck in the rendering pipeline.
words.exampleSentence: The complex blur filter caused a delay in rasterization, leading to frame drops during the transition.

codeExample.code:
// Rasterization turns "Draw a blue circle at 10,10 with radius 5"
// into a specific grid of blue pixels in memory.

words.shortDefinition: The primary thread where the browser handles most tasks.
words.longDefinition: In the browser, the main thread is where JavaScript is executed, the DOM is built, and Layout/Paint occur. If a task takes too long on the main thread, the page becomes unresponsive (the 'locked' UI).
words.techContext: The 'don't block the main thread' rule is the most important guideline for web performance.
words.exampleSentence: A long-running loop on the main thread prevented the browser from responding to the user's scroll input.

words.shortDefinition: A script that acts as a proxy between the web app and the network.
words.longDefinition: A type of web worker that runs in the background and can intercept network requests, cache resources, and handle push notifications. It is the core technology behind Progressive Web Apps (PWAs).
words.techContext: Used to enable 'offline mode' and significantly speed up repeat visits by serving cached content without hitting the network.
words.exampleSentence: The service worker intercepted the failed fetch request and returned a cached version of the homepage instead.

words.shortDefinition: A short task that should be executed immediately after the current script finishes.
words.longDefinition: Tasks usually created by Promises (.then/catch/finally) or MutationObserver. The microtask queue has higher priority than the macrotask (callback) queue; all pending microtasks are cleared before the browser continues to the next macrotask.
words.techContext: Understanding the difference between microtasks and macrotasks is key to predicting the execution order of async code.
words.exampleSentence: Even though the Promise resolved after the setTimeout was called, its callback ran first because it was a microtask.

words.shortDefinition: The automatic process of reclaiming memory by deleting objects that are no longer reachable.
words.longDefinition: JavaScript engines use algorithms like 'Mark-and-Sweep' to find objects that are no longer referenced by the program and free up their memory. This happens periodically and can cause 'GC pauses' in execution.
words.techContext: While automatic, developers can cause memory leaks by accidentally keeping references to large objects (e.g., in global variables or forgotten intervals).
words.exampleSentence: Frequent garbage collection pauses were causing our animation to stutter, so we reduced object allocation in our render loop.

words.shortDefinition: A queue where async callbacks wait to be executed by the event loop.
words.longDefinition: Also known as the Task Queue or Macrotask Queue. When an async operation (like a timer or network request) completes, its callback is placed here. The Event Loop moves these callbacks to the Call Stack one by one, but only when the stack is empty.
words.techContext: The 'waiting room' for async events. If the main thread is busy, items will pile up in the callback queue, leading to perceived lag.
words.exampleSentence: The 'click' event handler was stuck in the callback queue because a heavy calculation was still occupying the call stack.

words.shortDefinition: A type of web worker that can be accessed from several browsing contexts.
words.longDefinition: Unlike a regular Web Worker which is tied to a single tab, a Shared Worker can be used by multiple tabs, iframes, or windows from the same origin. They are useful for coordinating state or connections across tabs.
words.techContext: Ideal for maintaining a single WebSocket connection that is shared by all open tabs of an application.
words.exampleSentence: We used a Shared Worker to synchronize the notification count across all three open browser tabs.

words.shortDefinition: The process of filling in pixels for each element.
words.longDefinition: After Layout determines where elements go, Painting determines what they look like. It involves drawing text, colors, images, borders, and shadows. The browser creates 'display items' which are essentially a list of drawing instructions.
words.techContext: Complex CSS effects like box-shadow or filters increase the time spent in the Painting phase.
words.exampleSentence: By reducing the number of overlapping translucent layers, we sped up the Painting phase of our app.
words.etymology: Metaphor for 'painting' visuals onto a canvas.

words.shortDefinition: A browser API for collecting detailed network timing data for resources.
words.longDefinition: Provides a way for developers to programmatically access precise timestamps for the loading of resources (scripts, images, fetch requests). It includes data for DNS lookup, TCP handshake, and Time to First Byte (TTFB).
words.techContext: Used by monitoring tools to identify which third-party scripts are slowing down the page.
words.exampleSentence: Using the Resource Timing API, I discovered that our CDN was taking 200ms just for the DNS lookup in some regions.

codeExample.code:
const resources = performance.getEntriesByType('resource');
console.log(resources[0].duration);

words.shortDefinition: An API providing data about the performance of a page navigation.
words.longDefinition: Measures the time it takes for the entire page to load, from the initial request until the 'load' event is fired. It covers redirect time, app cache lookup, and DOM processing time.
words.techContext: Fundamental for calculating 'Time to Interactive' and 'DOMContentLoaded' metrics in production.
words.exampleSentence: Navigation Timing data showed that users on slow 3G were waiting over 10 seconds for the DOM to become interactive.

codeExample.code:
const [nav] = performance.getEntriesByType('navigation');
console.log('DOM ready in:', nav.domInteractive);

words.shortDefinition: An API for measuring custom application-specific performance metrics.
words.longDefinition: Allows developers to create high-resolution timestamps ('marks') and measure the duration between them ('measures'). These appear in the browser's performance timeline.
words.techContext: Used to measure how long a specific action (like 'Open Modal' or 'Submit Form') takes within the app.
words.exampleSentence: I added a User Timing mark before the complex data sort so we could measure exactly how long it takes in production.

words.shortDefinition: A binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine.
words.longDefinition: Often abbreviated as Wasm. It is designed as a portable compilation target for programming languages like C, C++, and Rust, enabling deployment on the web for client and server applications at near-native speed.
words.techContext: Used for performance-intensive tasks like video editing, 3D games, or cryptography within the browser, running alongside JavaScript.
words.exampleSentence: By porting our image processing library to WebAssembly, we improved processing speed by 10x compared to the pure JS implementation.

words.shortDefinition: The browser phase where it calculates the position and size of each node in the render tree.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Reflow. It's a recursive process where the browser starts from the root and works down to determine where every element should be placed on the screen based on the box model and computed styles.
words.techContext: A critical part of the rendering pipeline. Complex layouts or frequent DOM mutations can make this phase a bottleneck.
words.exampleSentence: The layout phase revealed that the sidebar was overlapping with the main content due to a misconfigured CSS grid.
words.etymology: Descriptive of laying out elements on a page.
